dataset/redundancy:
* **Redundancy Methodology**: The authors prioritized studies with larger sample sizes to bolster the representativeness and robustness of their analysis. When studies developed multiple models within the same data modality using the same dataset, the model with the best performance was selected rather than averaging the performance. This decision aimed to standardize the best-performing models for each dataset before assessing the moderating effect of the covariates.

* **Independent Sets**: Yes. The authors explicitly mention that they prioritized studies with larger sample sizes and selected the best-performing model to avoid averaging, which could obscure the effect of covariates. Additionally, they conducted a unified analysis for internal and external validation to ensure consistency.

* **Independence Enforcement**: The authors selected the model with the best performance rather than averaging the performance of multiple models within the same data modality. This approach was taken to standardize the best-performing models for each dataset before assessing the moderating effect of the covariates. They also prioritized studies with larger sample sizes to ensure representativeness and robustness.

evaluation/comparison:
* **Comparison Performed**: Yes
  * **List of tools/baselines**:
    * **Filter Methods**: Compared to embedded and multivariate techniques.
    * **Deep Learning (DL) Methods**: Compared to traditional machine learning methods.
    * **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)**: Compared to logistic regression (LR).
    * **Internal Validation**: Compared to external validation using an independent dataset.
    * **Algorithm Types**: Compared seven types of algorithms (CNN, DNN, SVM, Gradient Boosting, Decision Tree, LDA, and LR).
evaluation/confidence:
* **Statistical Confidence**:
  * **CIs**: Yes - 95% CI, 82.4% –89.6% (SE for filter methods), 95% CI, 80.3% –88.6% (SP for filter methods), 95% CI, 72.2% –78.2% (SE for no feature selection), 95% CI, 72.9%–79.9% (SP for no feature selection), 95% CI, 79.9% –89.6% (SE for DL methods), 95% CI, 79.8% –92.1% (SP for DL methods), 95% CI, 75.9% –80.2% (SE for no DL methods), 95% CI, 76.3% –80.6% (SP for no DL methods), 95% CI, 79.4% –94.6% (SE for CNN), 95% CI, 74.8% –94.8% (SP for CNN), 95% CI, 59.1% –76.3% (SE for LR), 95% CI, 62.4% –81.4% (SP for LR), 95% CI, 60.8% –76.5% (SE for external validation), 95% CI, 64.1%–82.3% (SP for external validation), 95% CI, 77.8% –81.8% (SE for internal validation), 95% CI, 78.3% –82.6% (SP for internal validation).
  * **p-values**: Yes - P = 0.042 (DUP), P = 0.001 (filter methods SE), P = 0.041 (filter methods SP), P = 0.001 (no feature selection SE), P = 0.014 (no feature selection SP), P = 0.018 (DL methods SE), P = 0.012 (DL methods SP), P = 0.031 (CNN SE), P = 0.004 (LR SE), P = 0.007 (external validation SE), P = 0.002 (publication bias), P = 0.005 (PANSS positive scores SE), P < 0.001 (PANSS positive scores SP), P = 0.017 (PANSS negative scores SP), P = 0.030 (PANSS positive-to-negative ratio SE), P = 0.008 (PANSS positive-to-negative ratio SP), P = 0.010 (GAF score SE), P = 0.006 (age SP), P = 0.039 (total sample age SP), P = 0.031 (age of onset SP).
